title: Mātauranga Rongoā: The Science of Ngāti Kahungunu Healing Plants / Te Pūtaiao o ngā Rongoā o Ngāti Kahungunu
subject: Science
culturalContext: Students will explore the medicinal properties of native plants found along the Mangakōtukutuku Stream, connecting to Ngāti Kahungunu traditions where this awa was historically used for healing and purification. The lesson integrates mātauranga Māori with Western scientific methods, examining how tūpuna identified and utilized rongoā plants through observation and experimentation.
learningObjectives: Identify and classify at least 5 native rongoā plants from the Mangakōtukutuku area using both Māori and scientific naming conventions
Analyze the chemical properties of selected rongoā plants through laboratory testing and compare findings with traditional uses
Create a digital herbarium documenting plant properties, traditional uses, and scientific analysis with proper cultural protocols
Present findings to local kaumātua demonstrating understanding of both mātauranga Māori and Western scientific approaches

activities.title: Whakawhanaungatanga ki te Awa
activities.description: Guided visit to Mangakōtukutuku Stream with local kaumātua to identify traditional rongoā plants, learn their Māori names, and understand their cultural significance and traditional preparation methods

activities.title: Pūtaiao Laboratory Investigation
activities.description: Conduct phytochemical analysis of collected plant samples using microscopes, pH testing, and simple extraction methods to identify active compounds and compare with traditional knowledge

activities.title: Digital Rongoā Herbarium Creation
activities.description: Students create a digital database using iNaturalist or similar platform, documenting each plant with photos, GPS locations, traditional uses, chemical properties, and proper cultural protocols for harvesting and use

activities.title: Whakapapa of Healing Knowledge
activities.description: Interview local rongoā practitioners and compare their knowledge with scientific literature, creating Venn diagrams showing overlaps and unique aspects of both knowledge systems

activities.title: Future Kaitiaki Project
activities.description: Design a sustainable harvesting plan for one rongoā plant that considers both ecological sustainability (scientific data) and cultural practices (tikanga Māori)

resources: Local experts: Rongoā practitioner Tāne Matthews (Ngāti Kahungunu)
Scientific equipment: Microscopes, pH test strips, plant press, digital cameras
Cultural reference: 'Rongoā Māori: A Practical Guide to Traditional Māori Medicine' by Rob McGowan
Digital platform: iNaturalist app and Hawke's Bay Regional Council plant database

tasks: Digital rongoā herbarium with minimum 5 plants documented
Laboratory report analyzing chemical properties of 2 plants
Oral presentation to kaumātua demonstrating cultural understanding
Sustainable harvesting plan with scientific and cultural justification

nzcAlignment: Living World: Life Processes Level 5 - Understand how plants function and how human actions affect them
Nature of Science: Investigating in Science Level 5 - Develop and carry out investigations using scientific methodologies
Nature of Science: Communicating in Science Level 5 - Use appropriate science vocabulary and Māori scientific terminology
Science in the New Zealand Curriculum: Recognising multiple knowledge systems including mātauranga Māori
